topic: discussion about press freedom index rankings

turns.content: did you see the new press freedom index rankings came out. I have thoughts
I'd love to hear them. What stood out to you?

colombia dropped again. which surprises exactly nobody who actually works in journalism here. but every time the ranking comes out the government acts offended like they can't believe it and then does absolutely nothing to change the conditions that caused the drop

That performative outrage from governments is such a common pattern — treating the ranking as a PR problem rather than a reflection of real conditions on the ground. What do you think is driving the drop this time?

same things as always. threats against journalists in rural areas, lawsuits designed to bankrupt small outlets, the economic collapse of regional media so there's nobody left to cover local corruption. and then you add the digital stuff — the troll farms, the doxxing, the organized harassment campaigns against female journalists especially. like the ranking captures some of it but honestly I think it UNDERSTATES how bad things are

The economic collapse of regional media is something that doesn't get enough attention in these discussions. When a local paper closes, it's not just a business failing — it's an entire community losing its watchdog. And you're right about the gendered dimension of online harassment. The index methodology has gotten better at capturing digital threats, but it still struggles to quantify the chilling effect — the stories that never get written because journalists decide it's not worth the risk.

EXACTLY. the chilling effect. that's the real number they can't measure. how do you count the stories that don't exist. how do you quantify self-censorship. I know journalists who are brilliant who have sources who have stories and they choose not to publish because they have families and they want to be alive. and nobody can blame them

Nobody can blame them at all. Self-preservation is rational, especially when the state can't or won't protect you. But it creates this paradox where the most dangerous places to report are also the places with the least reporting, which means the least accountability, which makes them more dangerous. It's a downward spiral.

Do you think the index itself actually changes anything? Or is it mostly useful as a reference point?

it's useful for advocacy. like when the FLIP or CPJ wants to pressure the government they can point to the ranking and say look, the world is watching. and it embarrasses officials at international forums which is sometimes the only leverage that works. but does it change conditions for the reporter in tumaco who got a death threat last week? no. absolutely not

That gap between international advocacy tools and on-the-ground reality is one of the most frustrating things about press freedom work. The ranking can create diplomatic pressure, and that has value, but it operates at such a different scale from the individual journalist facing threats in Tumaco or Buenaventura or anywhere else.

What do you think would actually make a difference at the local level?

real protection mechanisms that work. not the ones that exist on paper. actual bodyguards, actual relocation programs that don't take six months of paperwork while someone is receiving threats NOW. and prosecution. one single conviction of someone who ordered the murder of a journalist would do more than any index ranking ever could. impunity is the engine of all of this. as long as killing or threatening a journalist has no consequences, the rankings will keep going down and the speeches will keep being the same and nothing will change

Impunity really is the root. You can have all the legal frameworks and protection programs in the world, but if there's a near-zero chance of prosecution for attacks on journalists, those protections are just decoration. The signal it sends is devastating: journalists are expendable, and there are no consequences for treating them that way.

The conviction point is powerful — one real, high-profile conviction would shift the calculus for everyone considering threats against the press. It would demonstrate that there is, in fact, a cost.

anyway. rant over. I just get so frustrated when I see the number drop and I know exactly why and I know nobody with the power to fix it is going to do anything about it. but we keep reporting. que más se puede hacer parce

That's the thing — you keep reporting. That's what can be done, and it's not nothing. Every story published under these conditions is an act of defiance against the impunity you're describing. The index number is just a snapshot, but the work you and your colleagues do is what gives it meaning. Keep pushing.
